title: GPT-1: Proving Pretraining Works
concept: GPT-1 proved that unsupervised pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning could solve diverse NLP tasks, establishing the generative roadmap.

exercises.prompt: Which historical AI model first proved that reading raw text makes fine-tuning on specific tasks much easier?
exercises.explain: GPT-1 is the grandparent of modern AI systems like ChatGPT. It proved the concept of pretraining.

cards.title: The Great Shift
cards.body: Before GPT-1, AI models had to be built from scratch for every single task. Imagine having to buy a whole new phone just to use a different app! GPT-1 changed everything by proving one AI could learn general language first, then adapt to almost anything.

cards.title: What is GPT-1?
cards.body: GPT-1 introduced a two-step recipe: first, 'Pretraining' where it reads unlabeled internet text to learn grammar and facts. Second, 'Fine-tuning' where it is adapted to solve specific tasks like answering questions or sorting feelings.

cards.title: The Puppy School Analogy
cards.body: Imagine training a puppy. First, you let them roam the house, sniff around, and learn how the world works on their own (pretraining). Once they understand the basic rules of the house, you teach them specific tricks like 'sit' or 'shake' (fine-tuning). GPT-1 did the exact same thing with language!

cards.title: One Brain, Many Jobs
cards.body: Instead of building a separate AI for translating languages and another for summarizing news, GPT-1 used the same pretrained brain for both! It only needed a tiny bit of extra practice (fine-tuning) to excel at each job.

cards.title: The Reading Myth
cards.body: Many people thought an AI couldn't learn anything useful just by reading random books without a human teacher correcting every word. GPT-1 busted this myth by showing that 'unsupervised' reading makes the AI incredibly smart before it even starts school!

cards.title: The Paradigm Shift
cards.body: Before GPT-1, AI models were built separately for every task. GPT-1 introduced a unified approach where one model learns general language first, then adapts to anything.

right.text: Train one big model on general text, then tweak it for different tasks.

left.text: Build a brand new AI model from scratch for every single task.

exercises.prompt: Which two-step recipe did GPT-1 establish as the future of AI?

options.text: Unsupervised pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning
Supervised training only, with no reading phase
Deleting all data and starting fresh for every single task
Using human experts to write every response by hand

exercises.explain: GPT-1 proved that unsupervised pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning is a highly effective way to build language models.

exercises.prompt: True or False: GPT-1 showed that unsupervised pretraining helps an AI perform better on specific, diverse tasks.
exercises.explain: Yes! GPT-1 proved that learning from raw, unlabeled text (pretraining) gives the AI a strong foundation for many different tasks.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the GPT-1 roadmap.
exercises.sentence: GPT-1 first uses pretraining to learn language, followed by ___ to master specific tasks.
exercises.explain: First, the AI learns general language through pretraining. Then, it undergoes fine-tuning to master a specific task.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents the GPT-1 roadmap?

options.text: An AI that only reads books and never does any chores.
An AI that reads the web, then practices sorting customer reviews.
An AI that is built from scratch only to play chess.

exercises.explain: A pre-trained model needs to learn how to do specific tasks, which is what the 'fine-tuning' stage is for.

exercises.prompt: How did GPT-1 prove that the 'pretraining' roadmap actually works?

options.text: It only trained on labeled datasets from day one.
It read general text first, then practiced specific tasks.
It memorized dictionary definitions instead of reading stories.
It skipped the pretraining step to save time.

exercises.explain: GPT-1's breakthrough was using a two-step process: first, it read massive amounts of general text to learn how language works (unsupervised pretraining), and then it was trained on specific tasks (supervised fine-tuning).
